original_abstract:
Direct functionalization of methane into high-value chemicals remains a significant challenge in catalysis. Conventional industrial production of acetic acid (AcOH) involves energy-intensive, multi-step processes such as methanol carbonylation, which often require expensive homogeneous catalysts and aggressive reagents. Here, we report a novel photocatalytic route for the direct synthesis of acetic acid from methane (CH₄) and carbon monoxide (CO) at ambient temperature, utilizing water (H₂O) as the sole external oxygen source. 

The optimized photocatalytic system consists of a TiO₂ support integrated with ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) sub-nanoclusters anchored with isolated platinum single atoms (Pt₁). Structural characterization using aberration-corrected STEM and in situ IR spectroscopy confirms the atomic dispersion of Pt on the NPW clusters, which are highly dispersed over the TiO₂ surface. This catalyst achieves a stable production of a 5.7 mmol·L⁻¹ acetic acid solution over 60 hours with a liquid-phase selectivity exceeding 90% and a turnover number (TON) of approximately 99. Isotopic labeling and in situ EPR experiments demonstrate that the reaction proceeds via the activation of methane by water-derived hydroxyl radicals (•OH), followed by its oxidative carbonylation over Pt₁ sites. This study provides a sustainable, light-driven strategy for the mild transformation of methane into essential liquid oxygenates.
humanized_abstract:
Direct functionalization of methane into high-value chemicals is still highly challenging in catalysis. Acetic acid is one of the most important acids widely used in industries such as chemicals, pharmaceuticals, textiles and foods. Conventional industrial production methods involve multiple steps under severe conditions like high temperature and pressure via multi-component transformation including through the carbonylation of methanol which commonly employs expensive heterogeneous and homogeneous metal catalysts as well as strong oxidants. The direct production of acetic acid from methane and carbon monoxide at ambient conditions using only water as an oxygen source has never been achieved. In this paper, for the first time, we have developed a simple sustainable catalytic system to convert methane and carbon monoxide into acetic acid at room temperature.

An improved version of a previous photocatalytic system is disclosed, comprised of a TiO₂ support material that has ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) sub-nanoclusters anchored thereto with isolated platinum single atoms (Pt₁) disposed on the NPW clusters. The structure of the material is shown using aberration corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and the composition and structures of the material before and after use is illustrated using in situ infrared (IR) spectra. The catalyst produces, under photonic irradiation and over 60 hours, a solution of acetic acid (CH₃COOH) of a concentration of 5.7 mmol·L⁻¹ with selectivity exceeding 90% for liquid products and with a total turnover number (TON) close to 99. Using methane (CH₄) isotopically labeled with13C, together with in situ electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) studies, confirm that the oxidation of methane with water under light takes place by the heterogeneous activation of CH₄ by hydroxyl radicals derived from water (•OH) produced on the semiconductor material and by the subsequent oxidative carbonylation of the activated species over Pt₁.
